explicit_text: My name is Tokushige Kizuka, born in 1901 in Fukuoka Prefecture during the 34th year of Meiji. I came to America at age 16 to join my father who was managing an apple orchard in Watsonville, California. My early years were marked by separation from my parents and adjusting to life in the United States. I worked hard in the orchards and lived through many challenges as a young immigrant.

explicit_text: I was born in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan, and spent my childhood there before coming to America. Many people from my village later emigrated, and I knew several who also ended up in Watsonville and other places abroad.

explicit_text: My father fought in the Russo-Japanese War and returned victorious. I remember the celebration when he came back, including a long parade where I was carried with a big drum on a handcart. That event was a proud moment for our family and community.

explicit_text: I boarded the ship Tenyo Maru in Nagasaki to come to America when I was 16. The ship ran aground near Yokosuka shortly after leaving Nagasaki, so we had to stay on board for about a week before continuing. Nagasaki was the last port I saw in Japan before my long journey.

explicit_text: We arrived at San Francisco after about 17 days at sea, including the delay near Yokosuka. At the Immigration Office on Angel Island, we underwent thorough physical examinations before entering the United States. This was the first place I set foot on American soil after leaving Japan.
